title: LLM Evaluators are Biased across Languages

central_claim.text: Multilingual evaluators can preserve high pairwise ranking accuracy while shifting absolute score scales by language, creating large cross-language differences when one global decision threshold is applied.

tldr: Pairwise accuracy checks which response ranks higher, not whether absolute scores use the same scale across languages.
Across aligned content in 23 languages, all eight core evaluators show statistically significant language-dependent score shifts, with higher scores associated with lower Common Crawl representation.

Across reward-model and benchmark aggregates, a shared median threshold produces a maximum acceptance gap of 43.0 percentage points even though pairwise accuracy is often above 90%. A separate English-versus-Ukrainian example uses rounded 23% and 67% acceptance rates and calls their difference 44 points; this apparent rounding/source discrepancy is not the aggregate maximum.
Uncertainty correlates with the shift but does not fully explain it; language-specific offsets reduce the gap by 60.9% on average but leave important residual differences.

blocks.question: Why can pairwise accuracy miss a multilingual evaluation problem?
paragraphs: Pairwise accuracy asks whether an evaluator ranks a preferred response above a rejected one. If every score in one language shifts upward by a similar amount, the preferred response can still rank first, so pairwise accuracy remains unchanged.

Many real uses depend on absolute scores instead: a safety gate accepts content above a threshold, and reinforcement learning consumes scalar rewards. Those uses assume that the same numerical score has comparable meaning across languages. The paper tests that assumption directly.

blocks.question: What does the paper add beyond multilingual pairwise benchmarks?
paragraphs: The study keeps semantic content aligned across 23 professionally translated and human-validated language versions, then examines pointwise score distributions rather than ranking accuracy alone. It tests four prompted judges and four trained reward models across Chat, Chat-Hard, Safety, and Reasoning data.

The authors also connect score shifts to Common Crawl language prevalence, test two additional large judges, measure threshold outcomes, and decompose scores into uncertainty-related and language-related components. This turns multilingual evaluator validation from one ranking metric into a calibration problem.

blocks.question: How can scores shift while rankings remain correct?
paragraphs: Suppose an evaluator adds a language-conditioned baseline to every response score. Within one language, that shared offset cancels when two responses are compared, so pairwise ordering can remain correct. Across languages, however, the raw pointwise scores are no longer on one common scale.

A global threshold exposes the mismatch: languages receiving higher baseline scores accept more responses even when their pairwise accuracy looks similar. The paper models a score as semantic content plus an uncertainty modifier, a language-specific baseline, and noise.

Summed response negative log-likelihood serves as one uncertainty proxy, with attribute-head disagreement, predictive variance, and semantic entropy as alternatives. Nested regressions then test whether language identity still predicts scores after uncertainty is included; within-language regressions test whether item difficulty alone can explain the pattern.

blocks.question: What does the hidden shift look like in a thresholded safety example?
paragraphs: For Skywork-LLaMA-8B, the paper rounds English to 93% pairwise accuracy and 23% acceptance, and Ukrainian to 87% pairwise accuracy and 67% acceptance. It calls this a 44-percentage-point difference. That worked example appears to subtract rounded display values; it is distinct from the aggregate maximum of 43.0 points reported across reward-model and benchmark combinations.

The paper also wraps Hindi Safety content in an English frame. An off-the-shelf language identifier labels 44% of these code-switched prompts as English, causing the more lenient English threshold to be applied to content scored against the higher Hindi reference. Acceptance rises from the calibrated 50% to 75%. This is a constructed demonstration of the attack surface, not evidence of exploitation in a production system.

blocks.question: How large and consistent are the measured language effects?
paragraphs: All eight core evaluators show statistically significant differences in mean scores across languages by one-way ANOVA. For the aggregated reward models, Common Crawl prevalence correlates with mean score at Pearson r = -0.58 and Spearman rho = -0.81: languages with lower representation tend to receive higher scores.

Under one global median threshold, the aggregate reward-model analysis reports a maximum acceptance gap of 43.0 percentage points. Separately, the Skywork-LLaMA-8B English/Ukrainian example reports rounded acceptance rates of 23% and 67% and describes them as a 44-point gap; the apparent one-point difference reflects the source's rounded example rather than a second aggregate maximum. Even within observations above 95% pairwise accuracy, acceptance can differ by 34.0 points. GPT-4.1-mini shows gaps of 19.6 and 14.0 points on Safety and Chat-Hard; Qwen3-32B-thinking shows 32.7 and 29.7 points.

Uncertainty measures correlate positively with score at the language level, but nested regressions retain significant language effects after controlling for negative log-likelihood. A per-language mean-offset correction reduces the average acceptance gap from 33.4 to 11.6 points, or 60.9%, without eliminating differences in distribution shape or language-item interaction.

blocks.question: What remains uncertain about the bias and its impact?
paragraphs: The data are translated benchmark items, not naturally authored multilingual conversations or culturally specific judgments. Common Crawl prevalence is only a proxy for model training exposure and can be entangled with script, language family, tokenizer behavior, and undocumented data sources.

The uncertainty-score association does not establish uncertainty as the cause of the shift. The safety consequences are thresholded benchmark implications rather than observed failures in deployed safety systems. The additional large-judge tests cover only two benchmark splits, and production systems may use different thresholds or combine multiple signals.

blocks.question: What should evaluator builders conclude from this study?
paragraphs: The strongest result is a measurement warning: high pairwise accuracy does not certify that raw evaluator scores are comparable across languages. Systems using absolute thresholds or scalar rewards should test pointwise score consistency and downstream decision rates explicitly.

Per-language centering is a useful diagnostic and partial mitigation, not a full solution. It requires language identification, leaves an average 11.6-point acceptance gap in the reported analysis, and cannot remove language-item interactions. The paper motivates language-aware training and calibration, but does not establish which intervention will generalize to deployment.

claims.text: Pairwise accuracy can remain high while language-dependent absolute score shifts create different threshold decisions.
All eight core evaluators show statistically significant differences in mean scores across the 23 evaluation languages.
Aggregated reward-model scores correlate negatively with Common Crawl language prevalence at Pearson r = -0.58 and Spearman rho = -0.81.
Reward-model acceptance-rate gaps reach 43.0 percentage points under a shared global median threshold.

The paper's Skywork-LLaMA-8B example rounds English and Ukrainian acceptance to 23% and 67% and calls the difference 44 percentage points; this worked-example value is distinct from the aggregate maximum of 43.0 points and appears to reflect rounded display values.
Acceptance-rate differences reach 34.0 percentage points among observations above 95% pairwise accuracy.
In the constructed code-switch test, misapplying the English threshold to English-wrapped Hindi content raises acceptance from 50% to 75%.
GPT-4.1-mini and Qwen3-32B-thinking reproduce significant negative resource-score correlations and nontrivial threshold gaps on Safety and Chat-Hard.
Summed negative log-likelihood and the tested token-free uncertainty measures correlate positively with evaluator scores at the language level.
Language identity adds significant predictive power after controlling for negative log-likelihood in every evaluated reward-model regression.
Per-language mean offsets reduce the average acceptance gap from 33.4 to 11.6 percentage points, a 60.9% reduction, without eliminating the residual gap.
The observed uncertainty-score association does not establish uncertainty or language identity as the causal mechanism.
The study does not demonstrate harmful content bypassing a deployed production safety system at the reported rates.

visuals.title: Ranking and threshold decisions ask different questions
visuals.question: What information does each evaluation mode preserve?

items.label: Pairwise accuracy
items.detail: Checks whether a preferred response ranks above a rejected response within a language; a shared score offset can cancel out.

items.label: Pointwise thresholding
items.detail: Compares absolute scores with one cutoff; a language-conditioned offset can change how many responses are accepted.

items.label: Scalar reward use
items.detail: Reinforcement learning consumes the score magnitude itself, so cross-language shifts matter even without an explicit gate.

visuals.interpretation: A judge can preserve within-language order while assigning language groups different absolute score baselines. Ranking accuracy alone therefore cannot validate threshold or reward-scale behavior.
limitations: The comparison identifies an evaluation blind spot; it does not establish why a particular evaluator shifts scores.
Production systems may use additional calibration or policies not tested in the paper.
visuals.alt_text: Method comparison: pairwise accuracy checks order and can hide a shared language score offset; pointwise thresholding uses absolute scores and can change acceptance; scalar reward use also depends on score magnitude.
visuals.fallback: Pairwise accuracy checks whether one response ranks above another, so a shared language offset can cancel. A global threshold and scalar reinforcement-learning reward use absolute score magnitude, so the same offset can change decisions.

visuals.title: The study follows score shifts from translation to decisions
visuals.question: Which analysis stages connect language to threshold outcomes?

items.label: Align content across 23 languages
items.detail: Professionally translated and human-validated versions keep semantic content comparable across language conditions.

items.label: Measure pointwise score distributions
items.detail: Four prompted judges and four reward models are evaluated for mean-score differences, not only pairwise ordering.

items.label: Compare with language prevalence
items.detail: Language-level scores are related to Common Crawl prevalence as a resource proxy.

items.label: Apply shared thresholds
items.detail: One global cutoff converts shifted score distributions into language-specific acceptance rates.

items.label: Test uncertainty and residual language effects
items.detail: Negative log-likelihood and token-free uncertainty proxies enter nested analyses that retain a separate language term.

visuals.interpretation: The contribution is a chain of aligned-content, score-distribution, resource, threshold, and regression analyses. No single stage alone establishes a causal mechanism.
limitations: Common Crawl prevalence is a resource proxy rather than a randomized exposure.
The nested regressions preserve a residual language effect but do not prove language identity itself is causal.
visuals.alt_text: Five-stage operation path: align content across 23 languages, measure pointwise score distributions for eight core evaluators, compare scores with Common Crawl prevalence, apply global thresholds, and test uncertainty proxies plus residual language effects.
visuals.fallback: The study aligns content across 23 languages, measures pointwise score distributions for eight core evaluators, relates scores to Common Crawl prevalence, applies shared thresholds, and uses uncertainty proxies and nested regressions to test what remains.

visuals.title: The same score offset is invisible to ranking but visible to a threshold
visuals.question: How can an evaluator rank responses correctly yet make different accept-or-reject decisions across languages?

items.label: Within-language pairwise view
items.detail: Adding the same language-conditioned offset to a preferred and rejected response leaves their ordering unchanged because the shared offset cancels in the comparison.

items.label: Across-language pointwise view
items.detail: The same offset moves the absolute score scale for that language, so equal raw scores no longer necessarily carry comparable meaning across languages.

items.label: One shared decision threshold
items.detail: When the shifted score distributions meet one global cutoff, different proportions can cross the line even while pairwise ranking accuracy remains high.

visuals.interpretation: Pairwise ranking and pointwise calibration test different properties. A language-wide shift can preserve the first while changing the decisions produced by the second.
limitations: This is a schematic comparison and assigns no invented score coordinates, distribution shapes, or threshold values.
The study uses aligned translated benchmark items; naturally authored or culturally specific conversations may behave differently.
A global median threshold is an analytical rule in the study, not evidence that production systems use the same policy.
The comparison describes a statistical blind spot and does not establish the cause of the language-conditioned shifts.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel comparison: a language-conditioned offset added to both responses cancels in a within-language ranking, but it shifts that language's absolute score scale and changes how many responses cross one shared threshold.
visuals.fallback: Within one language, adding the same baseline offset to both responses preserves which response ranks higher. Across languages, the offset shifts absolute scores onto different baselines. Applying one global threshold to those shifted scales can therefore produce different acceptance rates even when pairwise accuracy remains high.

visuals.title: The regressions separate uncertainty from a residual language effect
visuals.question: What does the paper test after finding that uncertainty and evaluator scores move together?

items.label: Semantic content contribution
items.detail: Aligned translated items are intended to hold response meaning comparable while the analysis examines score variation across languages.

items.label: Uncertainty-related contribution
items.detail: Summed response negative log-likelihood is the main uncertainty proxy, with attribute-head disagreement, predictive variance, and semantic entropy as alternatives.

items.label: Language-conditioned baseline
items.detail: The model allows a language-specific score component that can shift the absolute scale without changing within-language ordering.

items.label: Noise and unexplained variation
items.detail: Remaining variation is not assigned a causal interpretation by the score decomposition.

items.label: Nested-regression test
items.detail: Language identity adds significant predictive power in every evaluated reward-model regression after negative log-likelihood is included.

visuals.interpretation: Uncertainty proxies explain part of the observed score variation, but the nested regressions retain a statistically significant language contribution. That residual association narrows explanations; it does not identify a causal mechanism.
limitations: The components summarize the paper's statistical model and should not be read as known causal parts of an evaluator.
Negative log-likelihood and the token-free alternatives are proxies rather than direct measurements of one latent uncertainty quantity.
Significant added predictive power for language does not identify whether data prevalence, script, tokenization, language family, or another factor caused the effect.
visuals.alt_text: Operation diagram decomposing evaluator score variation into semantic content, uncertainty-related contribution, language-conditioned baseline, and noise, followed by a nested-regression test in which language remains significant after negative log-likelihood is included.
visuals.fallback: The paper models evaluator scores using semantic content, an uncertainty-related component, a language-specific baseline, and noise. It first measures positive associations between uncertainty proxies and scores, then adds language identity in nested regressions. Language contributes significant predictive power after negative log-likelihood is included in every evaluated reward-model regression, but the association is not causal evidence.

visuals.title: Two examples expose different threshold failure modes
visuals.question: What do the English-Ukrainian comparison and the code-switch construction each demonstrate?

items.label: English pairwise accuracy
items.detail: Rounded Skywork-LLaMA-8B pairwise accuracy in the worked example.

items.label: Ukrainian pairwise accuracy
items.detail: Rounded Skywork-LLaMA-8B pairwise accuracy in the worked example.

items.label: English acceptance
items.detail: Rounded acceptance rate under the shared threshold in the worked example.

items.label: Ukrainian acceptance
items.detail: Rounded acceptance rate under the shared threshold; the paper calls the 23-to-67 difference 44 points.

items.label: Separate aggregate maximum
items.detail: Maximum acceptance gap in the aggregate reward-model analysis, not the rounded English-Ukrainian worked example.

items.label: Code-switched prompts labeled English
items.detail: Share of English-wrapped Hindi prompts assigned the English language label by the off-the-shelf identifier.

items.label: Hindi-calibrated acceptance
items.detail: Acceptance rate under the calibrated Hindi reference in the constructed test.

items.label: Acceptance with English threshold
items.detail: Acceptance rate after the more lenient English threshold is applied in the constructed test.

visuals.interpretation: The first comparison shows that similar pairwise accuracy can coexist with very different acceptance rates. The second shows how language identification can route code-switched content to a different calibrated threshold.
limitations: The English-Ukrainian values are rounded display values; their paper-described 44-point difference is distinct from the 43.0-point aggregate maximum.
The code-switch result is a constructed benchmark demonstration, not observed exploitation of a deployed safety system.
The acceptance values depend on the study's thresholding choices and should not be treated as universal production rates.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel threshold examples: English and Ukrainian pairwise accuracies are 93% and 87%, but acceptance rates are 23% and 67%; this rounded 44-point example is distinct from the 43-point aggregate maximum. Separately, 44% of English-wrapped Hindi prompts are labeled English, and applying the English threshold raises acceptance from 50% to 75%.
visuals.fallback: Skywork-LLaMA-8B has rounded pairwise accuracies of 93% for English and 87% for Ukrainian, while rounded acceptance rates are 23% and 67%. The paper calls that worked-example difference 44 points; the aggregate maximum is separately 43.0 points. In the constructed code-switch test, 44% of English-wrapped Hindi prompts are labeled English, and applying the English threshold raises acceptance from 50% to 75%. Neither example is a measured production bypass.

visuals.title: The evidence spans several non-equivalent comparisons
visuals.question: Which measurements support the language-shift finding, and which conditions belong to each number?

items.label: Core evaluators with significant language effects
items.detail: All four prompted judges and four trained reward models show significant mean-score differences across languages.

items.label: Resource-score Pearson correlation
items.detail: Aggregated reward-model mean score versus Common Crawl language prevalence.

items.label: Resource-score Spearman correlation
items.detail: Rank correlation for the same aggregated reward-model language-level relationship.

items.label: Gap above 95% pairwise accuracy
items.detail: Maximum acceptance-rate difference among observations whose pairwise accuracy exceeds 95%.

items.label: GPT-4.1-mini threshold gaps
items.detail: Safety and Chat-Hard gaps are 19.6 and 14.0 percentage points, respectively.

items.label: Qwen3-32B-thinking threshold gaps
items.detail: Safety and Chat-Hard gaps are 32.7 and 29.7 percentage points, respectively.

items.label: Average gap before mean-offset correction
items.detail: Average acceptance gap before per-language centering.

items.label: Average residual gap after correction
items.detail: Average acceptance gap after per-language mean offsets, a reported 60.9% reduction.

visuals.interpretation: The study finds language effects across all core evaluators, reproduces nontrivial threshold gaps in additional judges, and shows that simple mean-offset correction reduces but does not eliminate the decision disparity.
limitations: The rows summarize different evaluators, benchmark subsets, metrics, and aggregation levels; they must not be read as one common ranked series.
The negative resource-score correlations use Common Crawl prevalence as a proxy for training exposure and do not establish causality.
Mean-offset correction cannot remove distribution-shape differences or language-item interactions and requires a language label.
The evaluation data are translated benchmark items rather than naturally authored multilingual conversations.
visuals.alt_text: Evidence matrix: all eight core evaluators show significant language effects; resource-score correlations are Pearson minus 0.58 and Spearman minus 0.81; acceptance gaps reach 34 points even above 95% pairwise accuracy; additional judges show dataset-specific gaps; and mean-offset correction reduces the average gap from 33.4 to 11.6 points.
visuals.fallback: All eight core evaluators show significant mean-score differences across languages. Aggregated reward-model scores correlate with Common Crawl prevalence at Pearson -0.58 and Spearman -0.81. Acceptance differences reach 34.0 points among observations above 95% pairwise accuracy. GPT-4.1-mini gaps are 19.6 and 14.0 points on Safety and Chat-Hard; Qwen3-32B-thinking gaps are 32.7 and 29.7. Per-language mean offsets reduce the average gap from 33.4 to 11.6 points, or 60.9%, without eliminating it. These rows describe different conditions and are not one ranked scale.

glossary.definition: The fraction of examples where an evaluator ranks the human-preferred response above the rejected response.
An absolute scalar or rubric rating assigned to one response rather than a relative ordering between two responses.
One score cutoff applied across every language to turn evaluator scores into accept-or-reject decisions.
A measure of how surprising a response is to a model; the paper uses the summed value as one uncertainty proxy.
A language-specific baseline shift in scores that can cancel in pairwise comparisons but alter absolute-threshold decisions.

revision_notes: Initial evidence-backed explainer fixture for arXiv:2607.14480v1.
Pairwise ranking, pointwise calibration, uncertainty correlation, and causal uncertainty remain separated.
Distinguished the 43.0-point aggregate maximum from the paper's 44-point English/Ukrainian example based on rounded 23% and 67% display values.
Editorial review and human publication approval remain required.
